content.text:
{The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.}{Psalm 19:1}
 {Nature is written in mathematical language.}{— Galileo Galilei}

When a system $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ has a unique solution (i.e., when 
$A$ is invertible), Cramer's Rule provides an elegant formula for each 
component of the solution.

content.title: Cramer's Rule
content.content:
Let $A$ be an invertible $n \times n$ matrix. The unique solution to 
$A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ is given by:
\[
x_i = \frac{\det(A_i(\vect{b}))}{\det(A)}
\]
where $A_i(\vect{b})$ is the matrix obtained by replacing column $i$ 
of $A$ with the vector $\vect{b}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using Cramer's Rule
content.solution:
The coefficient matrix and right-hand side are:
\[
A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\vect{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\]

First, $\det(A) = (3)(-1) - (2)(1) = -5$.

For $x$, replace column 1 with $\vect{b}$:
\[
x = \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}}{\det(A)}
= \frac{(5)(-1) - (2)(1)}{-5} = \frac{-7}{-5} = \frac{7}{5}
\]

For $y$, replace column 2 with $\vect{b}$:
\[
y = \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}{\det(A)}
= \frac{(3)(1) - (5)(1)}{-5} = \frac{-2}{-5} = \frac{2}{5}
\]

The solution is $(x, y) = \left(\frac{7}{5}, \frac{2}{5}\right)$.

content.title: Cramer's Rule in 3D
content.problem:
Use Cramer's Rule to solve:
\[
\systeme{
x + 2y + z = 1,
2x + 5y + 2z = 2,
x + 4y + 3z = 1
}
\]
content.solution:
$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\vect{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

Computing: $\det(A) = 1(15-8) - 2(6-2) + 1(8-5) = 7 - 8 + 3 = 2$.

$$
x &= \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}}{2} 
= \frac{2}{2} = 1 \\[6pt]
y &= \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}}{2}
= \frac{0}{2} = 0 \\[6pt]
z &= \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}{2}
= \frac{0}{2} = 0
$$

The solution is $(x, y, z) = (1, 0, 0)$.

content.title: Efficiency Considerations
content.content:
For practical computation, Cramer's Rule is **not** the most 
efficient method for solving systems. Gaussian elimination requires 
$O(n^3)$ operations, while Cramer's Rule with naive determinant 
computation requires $O(n!)$ operations.

Cramer's Rule is valuable for:

- $2 \times 2$ and $3 \times 3$ systems by hand

- Theoretical analysis and proofs

- Finding formulas for solutions in terms of parameters

content.title: Adjoint Matrix
content.content:
The **adjoint** (or **classical adjoint**) 
of a square matrix $A$ is the transpose of the cofactor matrix:
\[
\text{adj}(A) = [C_{ij}]\transpose
\]
where $C_{ij}$ is the $(i,j)$-cofactor of $A$.

marginNotes.text:
Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom 
and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how 
inscrutable his ways!

marginNotes.author: Gabriel Cramer (1704--1752)
marginNotes.text:
The renowned Swiss 
mathematician who introduced the rule in his 1750 treatise on 
algebraic curves.
